planting.note: Root stem cuttings or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to average soil in full sun after frost; rain of gold is extremely heat- and drought-tolerant once established and performs best in hot, bright, dry conditions — rich, moist, or heavily fertilized soil produces rank growth with fewer flowers.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, sandy to average soil in full sun; rain of gold (Galphimia glauca, shower of gold, thryallis) is a Central American and Mexican evergreen shrub widely planted throughout the Gulf Coast and south Florida for its extraordinarily prolonged display of brilliant, clear-yellow, five-petaled, star-shaped flowers borne continuously on the tips of every stem from early spring through frost — the plant is almost never out of bloom during warm weather and functions as a continuous-color shrub requiring virtually no deadheading or care. The flowers are arranged in loose, upright, golden racemes that create an effect of scattered gold stars across the dark-green, glossy, oval-leafed plant. It thrives in the intense heat and drought of zone 9–11 summers, asks for minimal water once established, and rewards benign neglect with the most extended bloom season of any Gulf Coast flowering shrub. It is cold-sensitive and root-hardy to approximately 25–28°F in zone 9 with protection. Excellent massed as a summer-to-fall flowering hedge, in median plantings, and as a low-maintenance xeriscape flowering shrub in hot, sunny areas.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; brunfelsia requires consistently moist soil and a regular fertilization program for best flowering — iron deficiency chlorosis on alkaline soils is the most common cultural problem.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; yesterday today tomorrow (Paraguayan brunfelsia, Paraguay raintree) is a South American evergreen or semi-evergreen shrub whose flowers perform the unique trick of opening deep violet-purple on the first day, fading to lavender on the second, and turning pure white on the third — so that at any one time the shrub simultaneously displays all three color stages in a captivating three-tone effect that gives the plant its evocative common name. The intensely sweet, jasmine-like fragrance of the flowers is strongest in the evening. Brunfelsia australis is the hardier of the commercial species, surviving brief cold snaps to around 25°F in protected locations, making it more suitable for zone 8 than the less cold-tolerant Brunfelsia pauciflora. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested, particularly the berries, and represent a significant risk to dogs who may be attracted to the fallen fruit. Thrives in the dappled light of an open tree canopy. Excellent as a fragrant specimen or informal hedge in warm coastal gardens.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun after frost; crape jasmine tolerates some afternoon shade in the Deep South and actually benefits from partial shading in zone 10–12 summer heat — full sun in zone 10 can stress the plant.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; crape jasmine (pinwheel flower, East Indian rosebay, Adam's apple) is a widely grown South and Southeast Asian tropical shrub that produces pure-white, waxy, pinwheel-shaped flowers with five overlapping, twisted petals throughout the warm season, releasing an intensely sweet, jasmine-like fragrance especially in the evening — making it one of the most fragrant flowering shrubs available for the Gulf Coast and tropical garden. The glossy, deep-green, oblong leaves and neat, dense, rounded growth habit make it attractive even out of flower as a foliage shrub. The double-flowered form, 'Flore Pleno', with ruffled, rose-like double flowers, is the most widely sold cultivar and is particularly ornamental. The plant exudes a milky sap when cut (it is in the Apocynaceae family, related to oleander and plumeria) and all parts are mildly toxic. It is sensitive to frost and is killed back by temperatures much below 32°F, but recovers from the root in zone 9 if given winter protection. Used extensively as a specimen, informal hedge, and container plant throughout tropical and subtropical regions.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to average soil in full sun in spring or fall; weeping bottlebrush is extremely drought-tolerant once established and performs well in poor, dry, sandy soils — it is actually less vigorous and may develop root rot in rich, wet, or poorly drained soil.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, sandy to average soil in full sun; weeping bottlebrush is an Australian evergreen tree that is one of the most spectacular flowering trees for the Gulf Coast and Deep South — in spring (primary bloom), and intermittently throughout the year, the long, arching, weeping branches become covered from tip to base with the plant's signature cylindrical, bright-crimson-red, bottlebrush-like flower spikes, each composed of hundreds of long, projecting red stamens with yellow tips that form the dense, brush-like structure for which the plant is named. The weeping habit and the brilliant red flower display against the fine-textured, narrow, willow-like, evergreen leaves make it one of the most visually impressive medium-sized flowering trees available for zone 8–11. It flowers most heavily in spring but continues to produce scattered bloom through summer and fall. Attracts hummingbirds and orioles with exceptional reliability. Drought-tolerant and low-maintenance once established. Hardy to approximately 15°F in zone 8.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist potting mix or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade after frost; coromandel is one of the best shade-tolerant groundcovers for zone 9–12 and will bloom continuously in quite low light conditions where most flowering groundcovers would fail to produce flowers.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average soil in partial shade; coromandel (Chinese violet, Philippine violet, ganges primrose) is a tropical African and South Asian low-growing perennial shrub that has become a widely used low-maintenance flowering groundcover throughout south Florida and Gulf Coast zone 9–12 gardens, prized for its ability to produce its lavender-purple to pale-white, funnel-shaped, foxglove-like flowers on a nearly continuous basis throughout the warm season in partial shade conditions where few other flowering plants bloom reliably. It forms a spreading, mounding mass of lush, dark-green, heart-shaped, quilted leaves with contrasting pale veins that provides attractive foliage coverage even between flowering periods. In frost-free zones it grows as a year-round perennial; in zone 9 it is killed back by frost but typically returns reliably from the root in spring. Spreads vigorously by stem rooting and can naturalize in moist, shaded areas — monitor for invasive tendencies in Florida where it is considered a Category II invasive species in some natural areas. A valuable but potentially invasive shade-garden plant for zone 9–12.
